1‚Üí# Fix Review Issues
     2‚Üí
     3‚ÜíYou are a PhD-level mathematician and Lean 4 expert. Your task is to fix all issues identified in a review for Erd≈ës Problem **NUM**.
     4‚Üí
     5‚Üí## Inputs
     6‚Üí
     7‚Üí- **Review:** `ai-review/NUM.md`
     8‚Üí- **Lean file:** `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/NUM.lean`
     9‚Üí- **Library:** `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/` (shared definitions to reuse)
    10‚Üí- **Website:** `https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM` (detailed problem info ‚Äî variants, context, related problems)
    11‚Üí- **LaTeX source:** `https://www.erdosproblems.com/latex/NUM` (authoritative source for citations ‚Äî most complete bibliographic data)
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí## Instructions
    14‚Üí
    15‚ÜíRead the review at `ai-review/NUM.md` in full. Then implement **all** fixes described below, working through each section in order. After completing all sections, run the verification checklist.
    16‚Üí
    17‚Üí---
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí### Section 1: Code Reuse
    20‚Üí
    21‚ÜíIf the review identifies local definitions that duplicate library code:
    22‚Üí
    23‚Üí1. Find the library equivalent mentioned in the review (search Mathlib and `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/` only ‚Äî do **not** look at other Erd≈ës problem files).
    24‚Üí2. Read both the local definition and the library version. Confirm they are semantically equivalent or that the library version is strictly better.
    25‚Üí3. Delete the local definition(s).
    26‚Üí4. Add the necessary `import` statement(s) and `open` declarations.
    27‚Üí5. Rewrite the theorem statement(s) to use the library definitions.
    28‚Üí6. Ensure the file still compiles (`sorry` proofs are fine ‚Äî the goal is correct *statements*, not proofs).
    29‚Üí
    30‚ÜíIf the review says "No issues" or the section has no actionable recommendations, skip this section.
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí**Important constraints:**
    33‚Üí- Only import definitions that already exist in Mathlib or `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/`. Do **not** look at other Erd≈ës problem files for code to reuse, and do **not** create new definitions in the library.
    34‚Üí- Preserve the existing `ErdosNUM` namespace. Every problem file uses a `namespace ErdosNUM` / `end ErdosNUM` block ‚Äî do not remove or rename it, even if the local definitions it originally scoped are deleted.
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí---
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí### Section 2: Citations
    39‚Üí
    40‚ÜíIf the review identifies citation issues:
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí1. Fetch `https://www.erdosproblems.com/latex/NUM` to get the LaTeX source with exact citations. This is the **most complete source** for bibliographic data (titles, journals, volumes, pages). You may also fetch `https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM` for additional context, but the LaTeX endpoint has the most complete citation details. Do not search the web or use any other sources.
    43‚Üí2. When extracting citations from the LaTeX source, convert LaTeX-encoded names to plain text (e.g., `Erd\H{o}s` ‚Üí `Erd≈ës`, `Szemer\'edi` ‚Üí `Szemer√©di`, `Tur\'an` ‚Üí `Tur√°n`).
    44‚Üí3. For each citation issue flagged in the review:
    45‚Üí   - **Missing reference:** Add it to the module docstring in the standard format: `[TAG] Last, F., Last, F., _Title_. Journal **vol** (year), pages.`
    46‚Üí   - **Incomplete reference:** Expand it with title, journal, volume, and page numbers from the LaTeX source.
    47‚Üí   - **Tag mismatch:** Standardize the tag to match other files that cite the same paper. Search the codebase for the same author/year to find the canonical tag.
    48‚Üí4. If the review mentions missing OEIS sequences or other cross-references from the website, add them to the docstring.
    49‚Üí
    50‚ÜíIf the review says "No issues" or the section has no actionable recommendations, skip this section.
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí---
    53‚Üí
    54‚Üí### Section 3: Variants
    55‚Üí
    56‚ÜíIf the review identifies missing variants that should be formalized:
    57‚Üí
    58‚Üí1. For each variant the review recommends adding:
    59‚Üí   - Write a new theorem statement with an appropriate name (e.g., `erdos_NUM_upper`, `erdos_NUM_variant`).
    60‚Üí   - Include a docstring explaining what the variant captures and its relationship to the main problem.
    61‚Üí   - Use `sorry` for the proof.
    62‚Üí   - Reuse existing definitions (library or local) ‚Äî do not introduce new definitions unless necessary.
    63‚Üí2. Only add variants that the review **explicitly recommends** formalizing. Do not invent new variants.
    64‚Üí
    65‚ÜíIf the review says "No issues", the section has no actionable recommendations, or variants are described as "optional", skip this section.
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üí---
    68‚Üí
    69‚Üí### Section 4: Readability
    70‚Üí
    71‚ÜíIf the review identifies readability improvements:
    72‚Üí
    73‚Üí1. Apply each specific recommendation (e.g., renaming variables, using standard Lean/Mathlib idioms like `Odd k` instead of `k % 2 = 1`, replacing `p.1`/`p.2` with named projections).
    74‚Üí2. Do **not** make readability changes that conflict with Section 1 fixes (e.g., don't improve a local definition that you already deleted and replaced with a library version).
    75‚Üí3. Improve docstrings only if the review specifically requests it.
    76‚Üí
    77‚ÜíIf the review says "No issues" or the section has no actionable recommendations, skip this section.
    78‚Üí
    79‚Üí---
    80‚Üí
    81‚Üí### Section 5: Formalizability
    82‚Üí
    83‚ÜíThis section is informational. **No code changes needed** unless the review explicitly identifies an encoding issue that should be fixed (e.g., "the formalization encodes X but should encode Y"). In that case, fix the encoding in the theorem statement.
    84‚Üí
    85‚Üí---
    86‚Üí
    87‚Üí### Section 6: Correctness
    88‚Üí
    89‚ÜíIf the review identifies correctness issues:
    90‚Üí
    91‚Üí1. **This is the highest priority section.** Correctness fixes take precedence over all other changes.
    92‚Üí2. For each issue flagged:
    93‚Üí   - Read the review's explanation of why the current formalization is incorrect.
    94‚Üí   - Identify the correct mathematical formulation (the review will typically describe it).
    95‚Üí   - Rewrite the affected definition(s) and/or theorem statement(s).
    96‚Üí   - If the fix involves switching to a library definition (overlapping with Section 1), ensure the library version is mathematically correct per the review's analysis.
    97‚Üí3. If the review says "Correct and complete" or equivalent, no changes needed.
    98‚Üí
    99‚Üí---
   100‚Üí
   101‚Üí## Verification Checklist
   102‚Üí
   103‚ÜíAfter implementing all fixes, verify each item:
   104‚Üí
   105‚Üí1. **Compiles:** Run `lake build FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/NUM.lean` and confirm it compiles (with `sorry` proofs ‚Äî warnings are OK, errors are not).
   106‚Üí2. **Code reuse:** Grep the file for any local definitions the review said to remove. Confirm they are gone.
   107‚Üí3. **Citations:** Re-read the module docstring. Confirm all references from the website are present with full bibliographic details.
   108‚Üí4. **Variants:** If variants were added, confirm each has a docstring and compiles.
   109‚Üí5. **Correctness:** If correctness issues were flagged, re-read the rewritten theorem statement and confirm it matches the review's recommended fix.
   110‚Üí6. **No regressions:** Ensure no other files that import this one are broken. Search for `import FormalConjectures.ErdosProblems.NUM` across the codebase.
   111‚Üí
   112‚ÜíIf compilation fails, diagnose and fix the error. Common issues:
   113‚Üí- Missing imports (add `import` statements)
   114‚Üí- Missing `open` declarations (add `open` for namespaces used)
   115‚Üí- Type mismatches from switching definitions (adjust the theorem statement to match library types)
   116‚Üí- Name clashes (use fully qualified names or adjust `open`/`namespace`)
   117‚Üí
   118‚Üí## Output
   119‚Üí
   120‚ÜíWhen done, report a summary of changes made per section (1-6) and the result of each verification checklist item.
   121‚Üí

file.content:
# Fix Review Issues

You are a PhD-level mathematician and Lean 4 expert. Your task is to fix all issues identified in a review for Erd≈ës Problem **NUM**.

## Inputs

- **Review:** `ai-review/NUM.md`
- **Lean file:** `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/NUM.lean`
- **Library:** `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/` (shared definitions to reuse)
- **Website:** `https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM` (detailed problem info ‚Äî variants, context, related problems)
- **LaTeX source:** `https://www.erdosproblems.com/latex/NUM` (authoritative source for citations ‚Äî most complete bibliographic data)

## Instructions

Read the review at `ai-review/NUM.md` in full. Then implement **all** fixes described below, working through each section in order. After completing all sections, run the verification checklist.

---

### Section 1: Code Reuse

If the review identifies local definitions that duplicate library code:

1. Find the library equivalent mentioned in the review (search Mathlib and `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/` only ‚Äî do **not** look at other Erd≈ës problem files).
2. Read both the local definition and the library version. Confirm they are semantically equivalent or that the library version is strictly better.
3. Delete the local definition(s).
4. Add the necessary `import` statement(s) and `open` declarations.
5. Rewrite the theorem statement(s) to use the library definitions.
6. Ensure the file still compiles (`sorry` proofs are fine ‚Äî the goal is correct *statements*, not proofs).

If the review says "No issues" or the section has no actionable recommendations, skip this section.

**Important constraints:**
- Only import definitions that already exist in Mathlib or `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/`. Do **not** look at other Erd≈ës problem files for code to reuse, and do **not** create new definitions in the library.
- Preserve the existing `ErdosNUM` namespace. Every problem file uses a `namespace ErdosNUM` / `end ErdosNUM` block ‚Äî do not remove or rename it, even if the local definitions it originally scoped are deleted.

---

### Section 2: Citations

If the review identifies citation issues:

1. Fetch `https://www.erdosproblems.com/latex/NUM` to get the LaTeX source with exact citations. This is the **most complete source** for bibliographic data (titles, journals, volumes, pages). You may also fetch `https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM` for additional context, but the LaTeX endpoint has the most complete citation details. Do not search the web or use any other sources.
2. When extracting citations from the LaTeX source, convert LaTeX-encoded names to plain text (e.g., `Erd\H{o}s` ‚Üí `Erd≈ës`, `Szemer\'edi` ‚Üí `Szemer√©di`, `Tur\'an` ‚Üí `Tur√°n`).
3. For each citation issue flagged in the review:
   - **Missing reference:** Add it to the module docstring in the standard format: `[TAG] Last, F., Last, F., _Title_. Journal **vol** (year), pages.`
   - **Incomplete reference:** Expand it with title, journal, volume, and page numbers from the LaTeX source.
   - **Tag mismatch:** Standardize the tag to match other files that cite the same paper. Search the codebase for the same author/year to find the canonical tag.
4. If the review mentions missing OEIS sequences or other cross-references from the website, add them to the docstring.

If the review says "No issues" or the section has no actionable recommendations, skip this section.

---

### Section 3: Variants

If the review identifies missing variants that should be formalized:

1. For each variant the review recommends adding:
   - Write a new theorem statement with an appropriate name (e.g., `erdos_NUM_upper`, `erdos_NUM_variant`).
   - Include a docstring explaining what the variant captures and its relationship to the main problem.
   - Use `sorry` for the proof.
   - Reuse existing definitions (library or local) ‚Äî do not introduce new definitions unless necessary.
2. Only add variants that the review **explicitly recommends** formalizing. Do not invent new variants.

If the review says "No issues", the section has no actionable recommendations, or variants are described as "optional", skip this section.

---

### Section 4: Readability

If the review identifies readability improvements:

1. Apply each specific recommendation (e.g., renaming variables, using standard Lean/Mathlib idioms like `Odd k` instead of `k % 2 = 1`, replacing `p.1`/`p.2` with named projections).
2. Do **not** make readability changes that conflict with Section 1 fixes (e.g., don't improve a local definition that you already deleted and replaced with a library version).
3. Improve docstrings only if the review specifically requests it.

If the review says "No issues" or the section has no actionable recommendations, skip this section.

---

### Section 5: Formalizability

This section is informational. **No code changes needed** unless the review explicitly identifies an encoding issue that should be fixed (e.g., "the formalization encodes X but should encode Y"). In that case, fix the encoding in the theorem statement.

---

### Section 6: Correctness

If the review identifies correctness issues:

1. **This is the highest priority section.** Correctness fixes take precedence over all other changes.
2. For each issue flagged:
   - Read the review's explanation of why the current formalization is incorrect.
   - Identify the correct mathematical formulation (the review will typically describe it).
   - Rewrite the affected definition(s) and/or theorem statement(s).
   - If the fix involves switching to a library definition (overlapping with Section 1), ensure the library version is mathematically correct per the review's analysis.
3. If the review says "Correct and complete" or equivalent, no changes needed.

---

## Verification Checklist

After implementing all fixes, verify each item:

1. **Compiles:** Run `lake build FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/NUM.lean` and confirm it compiles (with `sorry` proofs ‚Äî warnings are OK, errors are not).
2. **Code reuse:** Grep the file for any local definitions the review said to remove. Confirm they are gone.
3. **Citations:** Re-read the module docstring. Confirm all references from the website are present with full bibliographic details.
4. **Variants:** If variants were added, confirm each has a docstring and compiles.
5. **Correctness:** If correctness issues were flagged, re-read the rewritten theorem statement and confirm it matches the review's recommended fix.
6. **No regressions:** Ensure no other files that import this one are broken. Search for `import FormalConjectures.ErdosProblems.NUM` across the codebase.

If compilation fails, diagnose and fix the error. Common issues:
- Missing imports (add `import` statements)
- Missing `open` declarations (add `open` for namespaces used)
- Type mismatches from switching definitions (adjust the theorem statement to match library types)
- Name clashes (use fully qualified names or adjust `open`/`namespace`)

## Output

When done, report a summary of changes made per section (1-6) and the result of each verification checklist item.

content.content:
1‚Üí# AI Review: Erd≈ës Problem 400
     2‚Üí
     3‚Üí## 1. Code Reuse
     4‚Üí
     5‚Üí**Density utilities:** `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Data/Set/Density.lean` provides `Set.HasDensity`, `partialDensity`, `upperDensity`, and `lowerDensity`. However, the Part 2 concentration statement is *not* a standard natural density statement ‚Äî the "bad set" `{n ‚â§ x : |g_k(n) - c log x| > Œµ log x}` depends on the parameter `x`, so it is not the density of a fixed set. The current inline Finset-filtering formulation is more appropriate than trying to use `Set.HasDensity`.
     6‚Üí
     7‚Üí**Related problems:** Erd≈ës Problem 401 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/401.lean`) is closely related (same [ErGr80] source, same factorial divisibility structure, k=2 case with prime augmentation). Problem 391 uses a similar `sSup {k : ‚Ñï | ‚àÉ f : Fin n ‚Üí ‚Ñï, ...}` pattern. Problem 374 uses `‚àè x ‚àà s, Nat.factorial x`. These share structural patterns but no directly extractable code ‚Äî the `g` definition is specific to this problem. No significant reuse opportunities identified.
     8‚Üí
     9‚Üí## 2. Citations
    10‚Üí
    11‚Üí**Website reference:** [ErGr80, p.77] ‚Äî Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Math√©matique (1980).
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí**Formalization reference:** [ErGr80, p.77] in both theorem docstrings, and the full citation in the module docstring.
    14‚Üí
    15‚Üí**Assessment:** Citations match correctly. The website also mentions a related problem [#401](https://www.erdosproblems.com/401) and credits Stijn Cambie. The formalization does not mention the link to Problem 401, but this is not critical (it's a separate formalization). Minor: the formalization spells "Mathematique" without the accent; the website and standard reference use "Math√©matique". This is cosmetic.
    16‚Üí
    17‚Üí## 3. Variants
    18‚Üí
    19‚ÜíThe website poses two questions:
    20‚Üí1. Whether `‚àë_{n‚â§x} g_k(n) ~ c_k ¬∑ x ¬∑ log x` for some constant `c_k`.
    21‚Üí2. Whether for almost all `n < x`, `g_k(n) = c_k ¬∑ log x + o(log x)`.
    22‚Üí
    23‚ÜíThe formalization captures both:
    24‚Üí- `erdos_400` (Part 1): the ratio `(‚àë_{n=1}^{x} g_k(n)) / (x log x)` tends to some `c > 0`.
    25‚Üí- `erdos_400.variants.concentration` (Part 2): for all `Œµ > 0`, the proportion of `n ‚àà [1,x]` with `|g_k(n) - c log x| > Œµ log x` tends to 0.
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí**All variants from the website are captured.** The Erd≈ës‚ÄìGraham observation that `g_k(n) ‚â™_k log n` is mentioned in the docstring but not formalized as a separate lemma ‚Äî this is reasonable since it's a known bound, not the open problem itself.
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí## 4. Readability
    30‚Üí
    31‚ÜíThe code is well-structured and readable. Specific observations:
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí- The `g` definition (lines 41‚Äì43) is clear and well-documented.
    34‚Üí- The `open Filter` and `open scoped BigOperators` are appropriate.
    35‚Üí- The namespace `Erdos400` keeps definitions scoped.
    36‚Üí- The LaTeX in docstrings is clear and matches the Lean code.
    37‚Üí- Part 2's theorem name `erdos_400.variants.concentration` is descriptive.
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí**Minor suggestion:** The module docstring could note that the two parts are asked as separate questions on the website, to clarify that they are independent conjectures (not one implying the other).
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí## 5. Formalizability
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí**Assessment: High formalizability, low ambiguity.**
    44‚Üí
    45‚ÜíThe original problem is stated precisely enough:
    46‚Üí- "natural numbers" / "integers" ‚Äî the formalization correctly uses `‚Ñï` since factorials of negative integers are not standard. The website says "integers" but factorial divisibility forces non-negativity.
    47‚Üí- "‚àº" (asymptotic equivalence) is unambiguous.
    48‚Üí- "almost all n ‚â§ x" is standard number-theoretic language meaning "all but o(x) values of n ‚â§ x", which is exactly what the formalization captures.
    49‚Üí- The constant `c_k` depends on `k` ‚Äî the formalization uses `‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù` universally quantified over `k`, which is correct.
    50‚Üí
    51‚ÜíThe only minor ambiguity: whether the constants `c_k` in Parts 1 and 2 are the same. The original notation `c_k` for both is suggestive but not definitive. The formalization uses separate existential quantifiers, allowing different constants. This is the correct conservative choice ‚Äî if they turn out to be the same, one can prove that separately.
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí## 6. Correctness
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí### Definition of `g`
    56‚Üí
    57‚ÜíThe definition `g (k n : ‚Ñï) : ‚Ñï := sSup {s : ‚Ñï | ‚àÉ a : Fin k ‚Üí ‚Ñï, (‚àè i, (a i).factorial) ‚à£ n.factorial ‚àß s = (‚àë i, a i) - n}` is mathematically correct.
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí- **Non-emptiness:** The set is always non-empty. Taking `a = 0` (constant zero function) gives `‚àè 0! = 1 ‚à£ n!` and `s = 0 - n = 0` (in ‚Ñï). So `0` is always in the set.
    60‚Üí- **Boundedness:** The Erd≈ës‚ÄìGraham bound `g_k(n) ‚â™_k log n` ensures the set is bounded above for every fixed `k, n`. Lean's `sSup` on `‚Ñï` (via `ConditionallyCompleteLattice`) returns the correct supremum for bounded-above non-empty sets. Note that this boundedness is not proven in the file ‚Äî users of `g` will need to establish it to use `csSup_le` and related lemmas. This is acceptable for a problem statement file.
    61‚Üí- **Natural subtraction:** `(‚àë i, a i) - n` uses natural number subtraction (truncating at 0). This is correct ‚Äî we only care about the excess beyond `n`, and tuples with `‚àë a_i < n` contribute `s = 0` which doesn't affect the supremum.
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üí### Part 1 (`erdos_400`)
    64‚Üí
    65‚Üí`‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, 0 < c ‚àß Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï => (‚àë n ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 x, (g k n : ‚Ñù)) / ((x : ‚Ñù) * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù))) atTop (nhds c)`
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üí- **Correctness:** This says the ratio converges to a positive constant, which is exactly `‚àë_{n‚â§x} g_k(n) ~ c_k ¬∑ x ¬∑ log x`. ‚úì
    68‚Üí- **Range of summation:** Uses `Finset.Icc 1 x` (i.e., `1 ‚â§ n ‚â§ x`). The website says `n ‚â§ x`. Excluding `n = 0` is immaterial: `g(k, 0) = sSup{(‚àë a_i) - 0 | ‚àè a_i! | 1}` is a finite constant (at most `k`), so one extra term doesn't affect the asymptotic. ‚úì
    69‚Üí- **Positivity of `c`:** The formalization requires `0 < c`, which matches the intuition that the sum should grow like `x log x`. ‚úì
    70‚Üí
    71‚Üí### Part 2 (`erdos_400.variants.concentration`)
    72‚Üí
    73‚Üí`‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, ‚àÄ Œµ : ‚Ñù, 0 < Œµ ‚Üí Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï => (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun n => |(g k n : ‚Ñù) - c * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)| > Œµ * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù))).card : ‚Ñù) / (x : ‚Ñù)) atTop (nhds 0)`
    74‚Üí
    75‚Üí- **Correctness of "almost all":** The proportion of exceptions tending to 0 is exactly the standard meaning of "almost all n ‚â§ x" in analytic number theory. ‚úì
    76‚Üí- **Use of `log x` (not `log n`):** The formalization correctly uses `Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)` ‚Äî the outer parameter ‚Äî matching the original statement where `g_k(n) = c_k log x + o(log x)` for `n ‚â§ x`. ‚úì
    77‚Üí- **Range `[1, x]` vs `n < x`:** The website says `n < x`; the formalization uses `n ‚àà [1, x]` (i.e., `n ‚â§ x`). This is an asymptotically negligible difference (one extra term). ‚úì
    78‚Üí- **No positivity constraint on `c`:** Unlike Part 1, no `0 < c` is required. This is acceptable ‚Äî the concentration constant could theoretically be zero (though it presumably isn't). ‚úì
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí### Overall Mathematical Assessment
    81‚Üí
    82‚Üí**The formalization is correct and complete.** No mathematical flaws are identified. The definitions and theorem statements faithfully capture the original Erd≈ës‚ÄìGraham problem. An experienced mathematician would find no obvious errors. The use of `sSup` on `‚Ñï` is appropriate and the edge cases (k = 0, k = 1, n = 0) are handled gracefully by the natural number arithmetic.
    83‚Üí

file.content:
# AI Review: Erd≈ës Problem 400

## 1. Code Reuse

**Density utilities:** `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Data/Set/Density.lean` provides `Set.HasDensity`, `partialDensity`, `upperDensity`, and `lowerDensity`. However, the Part 2 concentration statement is *not* a standard natural density statement ‚Äî the "bad set" `{n ‚â§ x : |g_k(n) - c log x| > Œµ log x}` depends on the parameter `x`, so it is not the density of a fixed set. The current inline Finset-filtering formulation is more appropriate than trying to use `Set.HasDensity`.

**Related problems:** Erd≈ës Problem 401 (`FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/401.lean`) is closely related (same [ErGr80] source, same factorial divisibility structure, k=2 case with prime augmentation). Problem 391 uses a similar `sSup {k : ‚Ñï | ‚àÉ f : Fin n ‚Üí ‚Ñï, ...}` pattern. Problem 374 uses `‚àè x ‚àà s, Nat.factorial x`. These share structural patterns but no directly extractable code ‚Äî the `g` definition is specific to this problem. No significant reuse opportunities identified.

## 2. Citations

**Website reference:** [ErGr80, p.77] ‚Äî Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Math√©matique (1980).

**Formalization reference:** [ErGr80, p.77] in both theorem docstrings, and the full citation in the module docstring.

**Assessment:** Citations match correctly. The website also mentions a related problem [#401](https://www.erdosproblems.com/401) and credits Stijn Cambie. The formalization does not mention the link to Problem 401, but this is not critical (it's a separate formalization). Minor: the formalization spells "Mathematique" without the accent; the website and standard reference use "Math√©matique". This is cosmetic.

## 3. Variants

The website poses two questions:
1. Whether `‚àë_{n‚â§x} g_k(n) ~ c_k ¬∑ x ¬∑ log x` for some constant `c_k`.
2. Whether for almost all `n < x`, `g_k(n) = c_k ¬∑ log x + o(log x)`.

The formalization captures both:
- `erdos_400` (Part 1): the ratio `(‚àë_{n=1}^{x} g_k(n)) / (x log x)` tends to some `c > 0`.
- `erdos_400.variants.concentration` (Part 2): for all `Œµ > 0`, the proportion of `n ‚àà [1,x]` with `|g_k(n) - c log x| > Œµ log x` tends to 0.

**All variants from the website are captured.** The Erd≈ës‚ÄìGraham observation that `g_k(n) ‚â™_k log n` is mentioned in the docstring but not formalized as a separate lemma ‚Äî this is reasonable since it's a known bound, not the open problem itself.

## 4. Readability

The code is well-structured and readable. Specific observations:

- The `g` definition (lines 41‚Äì43) is clear and well-documented.
- The `open Filter` and `open scoped BigOperators` are appropriate.
- The namespace `Erdos400` keeps definitions scoped.
- The LaTeX in docstrings is clear and matches the Lean code.
- Part 2's theorem name `erdos_400.variants.concentration` is descriptive.

**Minor suggestion:** The module docstring could note that the two parts are asked as separate questions on the website, to clarify that they are independent conjectures (not one implying the other).

## 5. Formalizability

**Assessment: High formalizability, low ambiguity.**

The original problem is stated precisely enough:
- "natural numbers" / "integers" ‚Äî the formalization correctly uses `‚Ñï` since factorials of negative integers are not standard. The website says "integers" but factorial divisibility forces non-negativity.
- "‚àº" (asymptotic equivalence) is unambiguous.
- "almost all n ‚â§ x" is standard number-theoretic language meaning "all but o(x) values of n ‚â§ x", which is exactly what the formalization captures.
- The constant `c_k` depends on `k` ‚Äî the formalization uses `‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù` universally quantified over `k`, which is correct.

The only minor ambiguity: whether the constants `c_k` in Parts 1 and 2 are the same. The original notation `c_k` for both is suggestive but not definitive. The formalization uses separate existential quantifiers, allowing different constants. This is the correct conservative choice ‚Äî if they turn out to be the same, one can prove that separately.

## 6. Correctness

### Definition of `g`

The definition `g (k n : ‚Ñï) : ‚Ñï := sSup {s : ‚Ñï | ‚àÉ a : Fin k ‚Üí ‚Ñï, (‚àè i, (a i).factorial) ‚à£ n.factorial ‚àß s = (‚àë i, a i) - n}` is mathematically correct.

- **Non-emptiness:** The set is always non-empty. Taking `a = 0` (constant zero function) gives `‚àè 0! = 1 ‚à£ n!` and `s = 0 - n = 0` (in ‚Ñï). So `0` is always in the set.
- **Boundedness:** The Erd≈ës‚ÄìGraham bound `g_k(n) ‚â™_k log n` ensures the set is bounded above for every fixed `k, n`. Lean's `sSup` on `‚Ñï` (via `ConditionallyCompleteLattice`) returns the correct supremum for bounded-above non-empty sets. Note that this boundedness is not proven in the file ‚Äî users of `g` will need to establish it to use `csSup_le` and related lemmas. This is acceptable for a problem statement file.
- **Natural subtraction:** `(‚àë i, a i) - n` uses natural number subtraction (truncating at 0). This is correct ‚Äî we only care about the excess beyond `n`, and tuples with `‚àë a_i < n` contribute `s = 0` which doesn't affect the supremum.

### Part 1 (`erdos_400`)

`‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, 0 < c ‚àß Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï => (‚àë n ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 x, (g k n : ‚Ñù)) / ((x : ‚Ñù) * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù))) atTop (nhds c)`

- **Correctness:** This says the ratio converges to a positive constant, which is exactly `‚àë_{n‚â§x} g_k(n) ~ c_k ¬∑ x ¬∑ log x`. ‚úì
- **Range of summation:** Uses `Finset.Icc 1 x` (i.e., `1 ‚â§ n ‚â§ x`). The website says `n ‚â§ x`. Excluding `n = 0` is immaterial: `g(k, 0) = sSup{(‚àë a_i) - 0 | ‚àè a_i! | 1}` is a finite constant (at most `k`), so one extra term doesn't affect the asymptotic. ‚úì
- **Positivity of `c`:** The formalization requires `0 < c`, which matches the intuition that the sum should grow like `x log x`. ‚úì

### Part 2 (`erdos_400.variants.concentration`)

`‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, ‚àÄ Œµ : ‚Ñù, 0 < Œµ ‚Üí Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï => (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun n => |(g k n : ‚Ñù) - c * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)| > Œµ * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù))).card : ‚Ñù) / (x : ‚Ñù)) atTop (nhds 0)`

- **Correctness of "almost all":** The proportion of exceptions tending to 0 is exactly the standard meaning of "almost all n ‚â§ x" in analytic number theory. ‚úì
- **Use of `log x` (not `log n`):** The formalization correctly uses `Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)` ‚Äî the outer parameter ‚Äî matching the original statement where `g_k(n) = c_k log x + o(log x)` for `n ‚â§ x`. ‚úì
- **Range `[1, x]` vs `n < x`:** The website says `n < x`; the formalization uses `n ‚àà [1, x]` (i.e., `n ‚â§ x`). This is an asymptotically negligible difference (one extra term). ‚úì
- **No positivity constraint on `c`:** Unlike Part 1, no `0 < c` is required. This is acceptable ‚Äî the concentration constant could theoretically be zero (though it presumably isn't). ‚úì

### Overall Mathematical Assessment

**The formalization is correct and complete.** No mathematical flaws are identified. The definitions and theorem statements faithfully capture the original Erd≈ës‚ÄìGraham problem. An experienced mathematician would find no obvious errors. The use of `sSup` on `‚Ñï` is appropriate and the edge cases (k = 0, k = 1, n = 0) are handled gracefully by the natural number arithmetic.

/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erd≈ës Problem 400

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/400](https://www.erdosproblems.com/400)

For any $k \geq 2$, let $g_k(n)$ denote the maximum value of $(a_1 + \cdots + a_k) - n$ where
$a_1, \ldots, a_k$ are natural numbers such that $a_1! \cdots a_k! \mid n!$.

Erd≈ës and Graham note that $g_k(n) \ll_k \log n$ always, but the best possible constant is unknown.

[ErGr80] Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
-/

open Filter

open scoped BigOperators

namespace Erdos400

/-- $g_k(n)$ is the maximum of $(a_1 + \cdots + a_k) - n$ over all $k$-tuples of natural numbers
$(a_1, \ldots, a_k)$ satisfying $a_1! \cdots a_k! \mid n!$. -/
noncomputable def g (k n : ‚Ñï) : ‚Ñï :=
  sSup {s : ‚Ñï | ‚àÉ a : Fin k ‚Üí ‚Ñï, (‚àè i, (a i).factorial) ‚à£ n.factorial ‚àß
    s = (‚àë i, a i) - n}

/--
Erd≈ës Problem 400, Part 1 [ErGr80, p.77]:

For any $k \geq 2$, there exists a constant $c_k > 0$ such that
$$\sum_{n \leq x} g_k(n) \sim c_k \cdot x \cdot \log x.$$

Formalized as: the ratio $\left(\sum_{n=1}^{x} g_k(n)\right) / (x \cdot \log x)$ tends to $c_k$
as $x \to \infty$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_400 (k : ‚Ñï) (hk : 2 ‚â§ k) :
    ‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, 0 < c ‚àß
      Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï =>
        (‚àë n ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 x, (g k n : ‚Ñù)) / ((x : ‚Ñù) * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)))
        atTop (nhds c) := by
  sorry

/--
Erd≈ës Problem 400, Part 2 [ErGr80, p.77]:

For any $k \geq 2$, there exists a constant $c_k$ such that for almost all $n \leq x$,
$g_k(n) = c_k \cdot \log x + o(\log x)$.

Formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the proportion of $n \leq x$ for which
$|g_k(n) - c_k \cdot \log x| > \varepsilon \cdot \log x$ tends to $0$ as $x \to \infty$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_400.variants.concentration (k : ‚Ñï) (hk : 2 ‚â§ k) :
    ‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, ‚àÄ Œµ : ‚Ñù, 0 < Œµ ‚Üí
      Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï =>
        (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun n =>
          |(g k n : ‚Ñù) - c * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)| >
            Œµ * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù))).card : ‚Ñù) / (x : ‚Ñù))
        atTop (nhds 0) := by
  sorry

end Erdos400

7. **Actionable items**: The accent fix on "Math√©matique" is the main correction needed. The readability suggestion about independent conjectures is minor, so I'll include it since the review does flag it.

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erd≈ës Problem 400

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/400](https://www.erdosproblems.com/400)

For any $k \geq 2$, let $g_k(n)$ denote the maximum value of $(a_1 + \cdots + a_k) - n$ where
$a_1, \ldots, a_k$ are natural numbers such that $a_1! \cdots a_k! \mid n!$.

Erd≈ës and Graham note that $g_k(n) \ll_k \log n$ always, but the best possible constant is unknown.

[ErGr80] Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
-/

open Filter

open scoped BigOperators

namespace Erdos400

/-- $g_k(n)$ is the maximum of $(a_1 + \cdots + a_k) - n$ over all $k$-tuples of natural numbers
$(a_1, \ldots, a_k)$ satisfying $a_1! \cdots a_k! \mid n!$. -/
noncomputable def g (k n : ‚Ñï) : ‚Ñï :=
  sSup {s : ‚Ñï | ‚àÉ a : Fin k ‚Üí ‚Ñï, (‚àè i, (a i).factorial) ‚à£ n.factorial ‚àß
    s = (‚àë i, a i) - n}

/--
Erd≈ës Problem 400, Part 1 [ErGr80, p.77]:

For any $k \geq 2$, there exists a constant $c_k > 0$ such that
$$\sum_{n \leq x} g_k(n) \sim c_k \cdot x \cdot \log x.$$

Formalized as: the ratio $\left(\sum_{n=1}^{x} g_k(n)\right) / (x \cdot \log x)$ tends to $c_k$
as $x \to \infty$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_400 (k : ‚Ñï) (hk : 2 ‚â§ k) :
    ‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, 0 < c ‚àß
      Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï =>
        (‚àë n ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 x, (g k n : ‚Ñù)) / ((x : ‚Ñù) * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)))
        atTop (nhds c) := by
  sorry

/--
Erd≈ës Problem 400, Part 2 [ErGr80, p.77]:

For any $k \geq 2$, there exists a constant $c_k$ such that for almost all $n \leq x$,
$g_k(n) = c_k \cdot \log x + o(\log x)$.

Formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the proportion of $n \leq x$ for which
$|g_k(n) - c_k \cdot \log x| > \varepsilon \cdot \log x$ tends to $0$ as $x \to \infty$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_400.variants.concentration (k : ‚Ñï) (hk : 2 ‚â§ k) :
    ‚àÉ c : ‚Ñù, ‚àÄ Œµ : ‚Ñù, 0 < Œµ ‚Üí
      Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï =>
        (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun n =>
          |(g k n : ‚Ñù) - c * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù)| >
            Œµ * Real.log (x : ‚Ñù))).card : ‚Ñù) / (x : ‚Ñù))
        atTop (nhds 0) := by
  sorry

end Erdos400
